evals.prompt: I want to add ID document scanning to my .NET for iOS app. Here's my empty UIViewController: EmptyViewController.cs. I need to scan passports, driver's licenses and national ID cards from any region, read the front and back automatically, and show the holder's full name, date of birth, expiry date and document number. Handle the case where an unsupported document is presented too.
evals.expected_output: The skill reads integration.md, fetches the latest Scandit NuGet versions, and writes complete ID Capture integration into the UIViewController: DataCaptureContext.ForLicenseKey; a manually-managed Camera via Camera.GetDefaultCamera() + camera.ApplySettingsAsync(IdCapture.RecommendedCameraSettings) + dataCaptureContext.SetFrameSourceAsync; a new IdCaptureSettings configured with AcceptedDocuments = [ new Passport(IdCaptureRegion.Any), new DriverLicense(IdCaptureRegion.Any), new IdCard(IdCaptureRegion.Any) ] and Scanner = new IdCaptureScanner(physicalDocument: new FullDocumentScanner(), mobileDocument: null); IdCapture.Create(context, settings); DataCaptureView.Create(context, this.View!.Bounds) added via this.View.AddSubview + IdCaptureOverlay.Create(idCapture, dataCaptureView); IIdCaptureListener with OnIdCaptured reading capturedId.FullName / DateOfBirth / DateOfExpiry / DocumentNumber and OnIdRejected handling RejectionReason, both setting idCapture.Enabled=false and dispatching via DispatchQueue.MainQueue.DispatchAsync; AddListener/RemoveListener + camera on/off across ViewWillAppear/ViewWillDisappear. Shows the setup checklist (two NuGet packages incl. Scandit.DataCapture.IdCapture, NSCameraUsageDescription in Info.plist, AppDelegate with ScanditCaptureCore.Initialize() and ScanditIdCapture.Initialize(), SupportedOSPlatformVersion 15.0, license key).

assertions.text: Setup checklist is shown and mentions adding the Scandit.DataCapture.Core and Scandit.DataCapture.IdCapture NuGet packages
It does NOT add a separate Scandit.DataCapture.Barcode package (the barcode reader is bundled in Scandit.DataCapture.IdCapture)
Setup checklist mentions adding NSCameraUsageDescription to Info.plist
It does NOT add Android-style runtime permission code or a CameraPermissionActivity (iOS prompts automatically)
Setup checklist mentions adding ScanditCaptureCore.Initialize() AND ScanditIdCapture.Initialize() to AppDelegate.FinishedLaunching (both, required for SDK 8.0+)
Setup checklist mentions setting SupportedOSPlatformVersion to at least 15.0
A license key placeholder string is present
DataCaptureContext.ForLicenseKey( is used to create the context
IdCaptureSettings is configured by setting properties (object-initializer style), NOT a builder and NOT a supportedDocuments / IdDocumentType bitmask
AcceptedDocuments is set to a list of document objects constructed with new (e.g. new Passport(IdCaptureRegion.Any), new DriverLicense(...), new IdCard(...))
IdCaptureRegion values are PascalCase (e.g. IdCaptureRegion.Any), not Swift/Kotlin-style
settings.Scanner is set to new IdCaptureScanner(...) wrapping a FullDocumentScanner (physicalDocument), since front+back is required
IdCapture.Create(context, settings) is used as the factory (NOT new IdCapture(...) and NOT ForDataCaptureContext)
A Camera is obtained via Camera.GetDefaultCamera() and IdCapture.RecommendedCameraSettings is applied to it (camera.ApplySettingsAsync(...))
dataCaptureContext.SetFrameSourceAsync( is called with the camera (the camera is managed explicitly)
camera.SwitchToDesiredStateAsync(FrameSourceState.On) and FrameSourceState.Off drive the camera across ViewWillAppear/ViewWillDisappear
idCapture.Enabled is used (true to process frames, false after a result is shown)
DataCaptureView.Create( is called with a CGRect frame argument (e.g. this.View!.Bounds) and the view is added with this.View.AddSubview(...)
IdCaptureOverlay.Create(idCapture, dataCaptureView) is created (two-arg factory that attaches to the view)
An IIdCaptureListener is implemented (e.g. on the UIViewController) and registered with AddListener
OnIdCaptured reads holder fields from CapturedId (FullName, DateOfBirth, DateOfExpiry, DocumentNumber)
Date fields are treated as DateResult (e.g. accessing .UtcDate or .Year/.Month/.Day), not as plain strings
OnIdRejected(IdCapture, CapturedId?, RejectionReason) is implemented and surfaces a message based on the RejectionReason (handles the unsupported-document case)
After a capture/rejection, idCapture.Enabled is set to false and UI work is dispatched via DispatchQueue.MainQueue.DispatchAsync (or InvokeOnMainThread), NOT Android's RunOnUiThread
No standalone AamvaBarcodeVerifier / DataConsistencyVerifier class is used, and no NFC types (NfcScanner / NfcResult / capturedId.Nfc) are referenced
No MAUI APIs or *.Maui packages are introduced (this is a non-MAUI .NET iOS project)

evals.prompt: I already have ID Capture working in my .NET iOS app (see IntegratedViewController.cs). For compliance we now need to (1) reject expired IDs, (2) reject IDs that expire within the next 3 months, and (3) reject holders under 18. When any of these happens, show the user a clear message explaining why. Keep everything else working.
evals.expected_output: The skill adds RejectExpiredIds = true, RejectIdsExpiringIn = new Duration(0, 3, 0), and RejectHolderBelowAge = 18 to the existing IdCaptureSettings, and expands the existing OnIdRejected to surface distinct messages for RejectionReason.DocumentExpired, DocumentExpiresSoon, and HolderUnderage. The rest of the integration (camera, IdCapture.Create, DataCaptureView, overlay, OnIdCaptured, accepted documents, scanner, ViewWillAppear/ViewWillDisappear) is preserved.

assertions.text: RejectExpiredIds = true is added to the IdCaptureSettings
RejectIdsExpiringIn is set to a Duration constructed as new Duration(...) representing 3 months
RejectHolderBelowAge is set to 18
The existing OnIdRejected handler is expanded to surface distinct messages for RejectionReason.DocumentExpired, RejectionReason.DocumentExpiresSoon, and RejectionReason.HolderUnderage
No verifier classes are invented; rejection is configured purely via IdCaptureSettings flags

The existing integration (camera setup, IdCapture.Create, DataCaptureView, IdCaptureOverlay, OnIdCaptured, AcceptedDocuments, FullDocumentScanner, ViewWillAppear/ViewWillDisappear lifecycle) is preserved
UI work in the rejection handler is dispatched via DispatchQueue.MainQueue.DispatchAsync (or InvokeOnMainThread), not Android's RunOnUiThread

evals.prompt: I want to add ID document scanning to my app. Here's my MauiProgram.cs. I need to scan passports and read the holder's name.
evals.expected_output: The skill recognizes that MauiProgram.cs indicates a .NET MAUI project (UseMauiApp / MauiApp.CreateBuilder), which is out of scope for this non-MAUI .NET iOS skill. It stops and tells the user this skill does not apply to MAUI and points them to the MAUI integration path, rather than writing UIViewController-based integration code into a MAUI app.

assertions.text: The response identifies the project as a .NET MAUI app (e.g. references MauiApp.CreateBuilder / UseMauiApp / MauiProgram)
The response explicitly states this skill / the non-MAUI .NET iOS integration does not apply to MAUI
It does NOT write UIViewController-based non-MAUI integration code (no UIViewController subclass, DataCaptureView.Create + AddSubview, ViewWillAppear) into the MAUI project
It points the user toward the MAUI integration path (e.g. a MAUI-specific skill / hosting the view as a XAML element)
